description: Claude Code plugin that captures user facial expressions via MacBook camera, detects emotions, and injects emotional context into conversations for empathetic interaction.

features.name: Plugin Scaffold & Project Setup
features.description: Create the Claude Code plugin structure with manifest, hooks config, MCP server config, and Python virtual environment with dependencies (opencv-python, fer, Pillow).
steps: Step 1: Create .claude-plugin/plugin.json manifest
Step 2: Create hooks/hooks.json with SessionStart and UserPromptSubmit hooks
Step 3: Create .mcp.json for MCP server registration
Step 4: Create requirements.txt (opencv-python-headless, fer, Pillow, numpy)
Step 5: Create setup.sh installer script (venv creation, pip install, permission check)
Step 6: Create package.json for Node.js MCP server dependencies
features.successCriteria: Plugin directory structure complete, setup.sh runs successfully, venv created with all deps installed.

features.name: Camera Capture Module
features.description: Python module that captures frames from MacBook camera using OpenCV. Supports single-shot capture and periodic background capture. Handles camera permission gracefully.
steps: Step 1: Create src/camera.py with CameraCapture class
Step 2: Implement capture_frame() - single shot capture returning numpy array
Step 3: Implement start_background_capture(interval_sec) - periodic capture to shared state
Step 4: Implement get_latest_frame() - retrieve most recent captured frame
Step 5: Add camera permission detection and user-friendly error messages
Step 6: Add graceful cleanup on process exit (release camera resource)
features.successCriteria: Can capture a frame from MacBook camera, save as JPEG, handle no-camera and permission-denied errors gracefully.

features.name: Emotion Detection Module
features.description: Python module that analyzes camera frames to detect facial emotions using the FER library. Returns structured emotion data (emotion label, confidence scores, face bounding box).
steps: Step 1: Create src/emotion.py with EmotionDetector class
Step 2: Implement detect(frame) - returns list of {emotion, confidence, bbox} dicts
Step 3: Implement get_dominant_emotion() - returns highest-confidence emotion label
Step 4: Implement get_emotion_summary() - human-readable summary string
Step 5: Add emotion history tracking (last N detections for trend analysis)
Step 6: Implement get_emotion_trend() - rising/falling/stable for each emotion
features.successCriteria: Given a camera frame with a face, correctly identifies dominant emotion from 7 categories (angry, disgust, fear, happy, sad, surprise, neutral) with >60% accuracy on clear faces.
features.notes: FER uses FER2013 dataset, 7 emotions. Library handles face detection internally.

features.name: Terminal Portrait Renderer
features.description: Renders user portrait in terminal. Auto-detects terminal capabilities: uses iTerm2 inline image protocol for iTerm2/WezTerm, falls back to ASCII art for other terminals.
steps: Step 1: Create src/renderer.py with TerminalRenderer class
Step 2: Implement detect_terminal_protocol() - check TERM_PROGRAM for iTerm2/WezTerm/Kitty
Step 3: Implement render_iterm2(image_bytes) - base64 encode + ESC sequence
Step 4: Implement render_ascii(frame) - convert to ASCII art using brightness mapping
Step 5: Implement render_portrait(frame, emotion_label) - composite render with emotion badge
Step 6: Add color support for ASCII art (ANSI 256 colors)
features.successCriteria: Portrait renders correctly in iTerm2 (inline image) and standard Terminal.app (ASCII art). Emotion label displayed below portrait.
features.notes: iTerm2 protocol: ESC ] 1337 ; File=[args] : base64-data BEL

features.name: Background Daemon Process
features.description: Python daemon that runs in background, periodically captures camera frames, detects emotions, and writes current state to a JSON file that MCP server and hooks can read.
steps: Step 1: Create src/daemon.py - main entry point for background process
Step 2: Implement capture-detect loop (configurable interval, default 10s)
Step 3: Write state to ~/.claudeface/state.json (emotion, confidence, timestamp, trend)
Step 4: Implement PID file management (~/.claudeface/daemon.pid) for lifecycle control
Step 5: Add start/stop/status CLI commands
Step 6: Add automatic shutdown after configurable idle timeout (default 2 hours)
features.successCriteria: Daemon starts in background, updates state.json every 10s with current emotion data, stops cleanly on signal. PID file allows hooks to check if daemon is running.
features.notes: State file approach is simpler and more reliable than socket IPC for hook integration.

features.name: MCP Server
features.description: MCP server that exposes tools for Claude to query user emotional state. Reads from daemon's state.json. Tools: get_user_mood, get_user_portrait, get_interaction_suggestion.
steps: Step 1: Create src/mcp_server.py using Python MCP SDK (mcp library)
Step 2: Implement get_user_mood tool - returns current emotion + confidence + trend
Step 3: Implement get_user_portrait tool - captures fresh frame, returns base64 image
Step 4: Implement get_interaction_suggestion tool - returns context-aware interaction advice
Step 5: Add health check / daemon status reporting
Step 6: Register in .mcp.json with stdio transport
features.successCriteria: MCP server starts via stdio, Claude Code can call get_user_mood and receive valid JSON with emotion data. get_interaction_suggestion returns appropriate advice based on detected emotion.
features.notes: Using Python MCP SDK (pip install mcp) for simplicity - single language stack.

features.name: Hooks Integration
features.description: Claude Code hooks that: (1) on SessionStart, launch daemon + capture portrait + render in terminal, (2) on UserPromptSubmit, read emotion state and inject as context.
steps: Step 1: Create hooks/session_start.sh - starts daemon if not running, captures portrait, renders in terminal
Step 2: Create hooks/user_prompt.sh - reads state.json, outputs emotion context as JSON
Step 3: Configure hooks.json with SessionStart hook (matcher: startup)
Step 4: Configure hooks.json with UserPromptSubmit hook
Step 5: Implement emotion-to-context mapping (emotion → system message advice)
Step 6: Add first-run experience: camera permission prompt + welcome portrait
features.successCriteria: Starting Claude Code in plugin directory shows user portrait in terminal. Each user message automatically injects current emotion context. Claude adjusts tone based on detected emotion.
features.notes: Hook output goes to Claude as additionalContext via stdout JSON.

features.name: Interaction Strategy Engine
features.description: Logic that maps detected emotions to interaction strategies. Generates context prompts that guide Claude's response tone and behavior.
steps: Step 1: Create src/strategy.py with InteractionStrategy class
Step 2: Define emotion-to-strategy mapping rules
Step 3: Implement positive mood strategy (maintain energy, celebrate progress)
Step 4: Implement negative mood strategy (encouragement, offer breaks, simplify explanations)
Step 5: Implement fatigue detection (drowsy pattern: neutral+low confidence over time → suggest rest)
Step 6: Implement strategy prompt generation (returns natural language context for Claude)
features.successCriteria: Given emotion=happy returns positive interaction prompt. Given emotion=sad returns supportive prompt. Given drowsy pattern returns rest suggestion. All prompts are natural and non-intrusive.
features.notes: Strategies should be subtle, not patronizing. Key patterns: happy→positive, sad→supportive, angry→calm, surprise→curious, neutral→normal, fatigue(trend)→rest suggestion.

features.name: Colored Pixel Art Portrait Renderer
features.description: Add --pixel mode to claudeface-vision Swift binary: face-crop via Vision bbox, block-average downsample with RGB channels, color quantization (6 levels/channel), ANSI 24-bit true color output using Unicode half-block characters. Stylized pixel art aesthetic like Claude Code's orange pixel avatar.
steps: Step 1: Add RGB struct and renderPixelArt function to claudeface_vision.swift (face-crop + downsample + quantize + ANSI render)
Step 2: Add --pixel [W] [H] CLI argument parsing in main section
Step 3: Compile and test with camera - verify colored pixel portrait output
Step 4: Update hooks/session_start.sh to use --pixel instead of --ascii
Step 5: Restore get_user_portrait MCP tool in mcp_server.py calling --pixel mode
features.successCriteria: bin/claudeface-vision --pixel 48 24 outputs a recognizable colored pixel art portrait of the user's face in the terminal using ANSI true color
features.notes: Core algorithm: Vision bbox → face crop with 30% padding → block-average downsample to pixel resolution → uniform quantize 6 levels/channel → ANSI 38;2;R;G;Bm fg + 48;2;R;G;Bm bg + ▀ half-block
